title: A Harvard Nutrition Legend on Saturated Fat, Dairy, Seed ...
text:
Walter, you've been at the center of
nutrition science for decades now.
Looking back over that time, what do you
think the field has mostly got right and
what would you say the field has has got
wrong?
>> Well, probably the field was most
offtarget when I started working in this
area. This was back in the 1970s. And at
that time, the main advice, the main
belief was that all fat is bad and
everybody should reduce all fats and
oils as much as possible. That was the
center of the food guide pyramid and
dietary advice when the pyramid first
came out here in this country in 1993.
And of course, if you're not going to
eat fat, it means loading up it on
carbohydrates. So that was the base of
the dietary pyramid. And the type of
carbohydrate wasn't distinguished. Lots
of products were made replacing fats,
even healthy fats with sugar or refined
starch. And unfortunately, that did
really have consequences.
>> So the focus at the early parts of your
career being on specific macronutrients
rather than the type of macronutrient
and the source of those macronutrients,
>> right? And as it turned out and a lot of
this was really known before but ignored
that the source and type of fat is
extremely important. we didn't really
understand so much about the importance
of the form of carbohydrate but since we
that time we have learned that that's
similarly important
>> and we'll we'll dig into both of those
things but at the at the top here as
we've as we've learned more particularly
over your career
so over the last 40 50 years or so has
nutrition become more complicated than
it needs to be
>> in some ways it's simple but some of the
nuances actually do turn out to make a
very important difference and uh we
still have a lot to learn and we haven't
studied all of the details of the
different sources of fat in the diet and
there's some micronutrients and
phyitochemicals that could make
important differences. So I think we
have the big picture fairly clear and
that's not too complicated but to have
the best information to be able to
provide the best advice. We're still in
a learning process.
>> People often say that nutrition advice
just keeps changing decade to decade.
scientists don't understand what a
healthy diet is. How would you respond
to that?
>> Well, I think one of the big prices of
the focus on avoiding all fats was in
fact that there did have to be a course
correction, a pretty major course
correction after that as the data came
in that the type of fat and the type of
carbohydrate are really important. So
that was a price to be paid and I think
it's good to acknowledge we were off
course. But the important point is
actually when I when I started in this
area, people were given advice, but when
I started to look at the evidence of
support for that advice, it just wasn't
there. It was best guesses at that time.
And so that's in fact why we started a
really long-term studies beginning in
1980 tracking approximately 100,000
women the nurs's health study at that
moment. And of course, you don't see all
the outcomes all at once. Early on we
saw that dietary fat really had very
little relationship if any to breast
cancer risk and it was thought there was
a really strong positive association.
But as evidence has come in, not just
from from our studies, from different
kinds of studies, studies done around
the world, actually there has really
been a strong convergence on what we
understand to be a healthy diet. And
that would be healthy sources of fat,
healthy sources of carbohydrate, and
healthy sources of protein in the diet,
emphasizing, but not exclusively
necessarily more plant-based sources of
protein and plenty of fruits and
vegetables. And I think actually there
is probably about a 90% agreement to put
rough numbers on it amongst
nutritionists that that's a healthy
diet. And if you look at the multiple
dietary quality scores, they're very
strongly correlated. Now there is a
problem though that's contributing to
confusion in the public's mind. First of
all, that among scientists, we don't
want to waste our time talking about
things that where we all agree because
we agree don't learn anything doing
that. So we spend our time and debate
about things where the the smaller parts
of the picture often that where we don't
agree and so because we publish things
and people are interested in diet that
exchange of ideas gets special
attention. But even worse than that what
people get in the press is usually the
least reliable information. If even the
the good press reporting accurately new
studies those new studies are the least
reliable. second, third, fourth
confirmation of the original finding.
That's really important. But that
doesn't get the headlines. The dog
biting man rather than the 10th study on
the man biting dog. That will get the
headlines, not the 10th study on dog
biting men. And to make matters worse,
we now have something called the
internet, which wasn't there when we
started. And that is just exploded
misinformation out there that
self-appointed. Anybody can say almost
anything they want to and grab
attention. And again, the most
sensationalist kind of claims are likely
to get the most attention. And you can
take almost any part of diet, say canola
oil, either it's toxic or it's going to
save you from quote infinite longevity
or something lots in between. So it's
really the wild west out there if one's
looking at the internet of information.
You alluded to the fact that in in the
kind of late 70s, early 80s, a blind
spot of the field was
that it's not total fat that that
necessarily dictates whether a diet is
healthy or not, but more so fat quality.
You know, how much of that fat is coming
from saturated fats and monounsaturated
fats and polyunsaturated fats and what
foods are those fats coming from? If you
were to predict today the dietary
guidelines that we have today, are there
blind spots, things that we cannot see
today and what would you predict they
may be that over the next 20 years we
learn that results in the dietary
guidelines changing even further.
>> Well, also to point out the huge blind
spot in 1980 was trans fat. It was
hardly mentioned. It was just off the
radar screen, but I was a bit worried
about that partly because I come from a
food science background and realized
what was going on behind the curtains
there. So, it literally was invisible
pretty much to most nutritionists. But
that's fortunately gone. And in terms of
our present blind spots, I guess if I
really knew they were blind spots, I
would be looking at [laughter] them, be
able to tell you about them. But I
don't. I don't think we'll have such
huge offtarget as we did before. just we
have so much more information than we
did when we started out.
>> Let me rephrase that question perhaps
cuz that's a difficult thing for you to
to kind of predict. Said differently,
what parts of our current dietary
guidelines are you least certain about?
>> You take almost every aspect and we
still don't have precise information
about how much. For example, just take
essential fatty acids, linoleic acid
critical. And there's some people out
there saying this is toxic and
pro-inflammatory. That's absolutely not
supported. In fact, we have a vast
amount of evidence saying that's not
true. Actually, polyunsaturated fats,
omega-6 polyunsaturated fats are not
pro-inflammatory. Uh there are many many
studies which show that. But still we
are not quite sure exactly what would be
the optimal range of polyunsaturated
fat. It's essential and for almost every
nutrient if you go to the extreme high
amount there'll be adverse consequences.
So this detailed mapping of the dose
response relationship for almost every
nutrient does need more precision to
sort of narrow our targets as much as
possible. But in fact the good thing is
that we don't have to be right on target
all the time. Human biology is
incredibly adaptable. That's one of the
conclusions that does come out of this.
But still, even though you can survive,
people would like to survive to the
longest age in general and have good
mental and physical function at the same
time. If you could rewrite the current
American dietary guidelines, is there
anything within within them today that
you would change or would you say that
if the public could adhere to them, it
would it would make a huge difference to
public health outcomes? Actually, you're
probably quite aware our guidelines are
about to change in a radical way within
the next few weeks as the RFK MA
guidelines state come out and they just
completely threw out the scientific
review that was published earlier this
year and are coming out with statements
that from what I've seen preliminarily
seem to have not only no scientific
basis but have scientific basis that in
fact refutes what the recommendations
are coming that will be coming out.
today looking at the well the official
guidelines came out 5 years ago there's
been a scientific report again earlier
this year but the official guidelines of
five years ago one of the areas where
there was I think less precision what's
the place of dairy in the diet and that
in reality is probably the most
interesting scientifically because
there's definitely lots of beneficial
nutrients there but we can see some
harms at the high end and then if you
put which is really important you put
the environmental impact impacts as part
of the picture. That's in the long run
that's probably the most important.
There's huge environmental impacts
depending on amounts of dairy production
around the world. So I think clarity and
more detail on the role of dairy would
be better.
>> Okay. I want to put a pin in planet and
eat lancet and we'll make sure we we get
there. But while we're here on on the
topic of RFK Jr. and some of the things
that have been said in the in the public
over the last month or so, particularly
this idea that updated dietary
guidelines will not have this kind of
emphasis on limiting saturated fat and
saturated rich foods. Do you think that
will cause harm to to the public? Are
you worried about that if that change
comes in into play? Well, at least what
RFK has said in public or it's been said
that he said that in public that
quotations that it's not so much
focusing on limiting saturated fat but
emphasizing that we need to increase
saturated fat and eat more beef and more
whole fat dairy and more butter and more
tallow and more hard and at the same
time saying we should decrease and
really try to avoid seed oils which are
the major source of polyunsaturated fats
in our diet. So, it's really that
coupling of promoting more saturated fat
and reducing polyunsaturated fat. That's
the real danger.
>> And what do you predict the the kind of
consequences of that could be if that is
the advice that that is communicated to
the American public? It's not just
advice because the dietary guidelines
are supposed to guide federal food
programs such as what children eat in
schools, what's available through
supplement programs and what's served in
US facilities, you know, millions and
millions of meals per day. And so we
know very well that if you replace
polyunsaturated fats with uh saturated
fats, tallow, butter, that that will
increase risk of coronary heart disease.
The premise of MA make America healthy
again is fundamentally wrong. That we
were living 10 years shorter lifespans
back in the 1950s and 1960s. We've made
great progress. Heart disease rates have
come down by about 80% since that time.
Now many factors were responsible for
that huge decline in heart disease rates
including not smoking or reductions in
smoking, better control of blood
pressure and some medical treatments.
But the change in fat composition since
that time has clearly been a very
important part of the progress we've
made. So we don't want to go backwards.
If you're to compare saturated fat to
something really bad, trans fat actually
looks worse. And or you could say that
saturated fats better gram for gram
compared to trans fat. And the best is
basically replacing saturated fat with
liquid plant oils and some mix of
polyunsaturates and monounsaturates like
olive oil or canola oil or soybean oil.
And so that's where you get the sort of
the you might say double win of reducing
the saturated fat and the positive win
more essential fatty acids that have
cholesterol-lowering effects and some
other beneficial effects as well. I
recently ran my full labs through
function health and I have to say the
results were eyeopening. Turns out my
apo was higher than ideal probably
thanks to a little too much coconut
yogurt. I also found out I was slightly
low in copper, something that I would
have never suspected without testing. On
the flip side, my biological age came
back 13.3 years younger than my actual
age, a calculation based on the work of
aging researcher Dr. Morgan Levine. So,
all in all, I've got a few tweaks to
make to optimize my lipids and nutrient
status, but overall, my blood work says
I'm doing pretty well. That's what I
love about function. You get access to
over 160 biomarkers covering everything
from hormones and inflammation to
nutrients, toxins, cardiovascular risk,
and more. And all your results are
housed in one beautiful platform, all
tracked over time. Once you get your
results, you can make informed changes
before small issues become big ones. To
get started, head to
functionhealth.com/simon.
The first 10,000 people get a $100
credit toward their membership. That's
functionhealth.comsimon.
When someone from the other side of that
argument positions this data, Walter,
usually the way that they'll position it
is to show the increased consumption of
vegetable oils and perhaps stagnation of
of animal fats or reduction in animal
fats. And they will show that alongside
data showing an increase in
cardiovascular
disease and an increase in type two
diabetes and an increase in obesity over
the last 50 years. So for someone who is
confused by what you just said and then
they hear that how do you explain that
there has been an increase in
consumption of vegetable oils and
polyunsaturated fats but at the same
time we've seen an increase in diseases
like type 2 diabetes and obesity. First
of all for heart disease heart disease
rates which are the target primary
benefit of reducing saturated fat and
increasing polyunsaturated fat that has
come down dramatically. In the last few
years though, that progress has
plateaued and maybe at the younger ages
a little bit of a hint of actually heart
disease rates going back up again. And
we've been really concerned about that
because of the explosion of obesity. And
it's amazing that in fact heart disease
rates haven't gone up faster than they
have given obesity is a very major risk
factor for heart disease. So we have a
mix of some good changes along the way.
Of course, trans fat has now been
eliminated. And so, there's been some
really important changes that have
benefited heart disease over the past 40
or 50 years, but at the same time, we've
have had a substantial increase in
carbohydrate intake that's been almost
all unhealthy carbohydrate. The biggest
single problem being a huge increase in
sugar sweetened beverages in the last
decade or so. That's sort come down
actually a bit. But if you look at the
bigger picture over time, there's been a
major increase in sugar sweetened
beverages and also a lot of unhealthy
carbohydrates in the diet. And of
course, again, diet isn't the only thing
going on. We've had a huge amount of
increase in activity, but we don't
really have very good data on that. But
someone who's, you know, grew up during
this period, I can tell you that when I
was growing up, there was one person two
blocks away that had a television and
with a 6-in screen and there was
essentially zero screen time. And now
it's it's massive both in children and
adults. And we can see that screen time
is very much related to increases in
overweight and obesity. And also kids
just went outside and played after
school and parents had to drag them in.
that doesn't happen much anymore for for
multiple reasons. So the physical
activity screen time part of the
equation has made a big contribution
clearly to obesity.
>> Right. So just to summarize that and
make sure that that's landing with me
and and the listeners, what you're
saying is since the kind of 1980s where
the low-fat message came out,
yes, we've seen some reduction in
saturated fat intake and some increase
in polyunsaturated fats. And that
probably has at least partly explained
the reduction in cardiovascular disease
alongside medications and just better
healthcare in general. But it's probably
not that change which explains why
people are more overweight, why there is
a higher incidence of obesity and type 2
diabetes. That's probably because
coinciding with that change in fat kind
of ratio distribution has been this
increase in overall calorie consumption
from a diet that's shifted towards more
ultrarocessed hyper palatable foods. And
also the other the other side of energy
expenditure is how much energy we're
actually utilizing per day and if our
activity levels are being reduced at the
same time that's also affecting our body
weight and and risk of metabolic
disease. Would that be a fairly accurate
summary?
>> Would although I sort of peg the
baseline back now late 50s early 60s
that's when the decline in heart disease
actually started. And during that
period, we've in fact about tripled our
increase in polyunsaturated pattern
intake.
>> I've heard you say that the
Mediterranean diet is one of the
healthiest kind of dietary patterns to
recommend to the to the public and often
a really good starting kind of point
foundation for people. Would you say
that's your favorite dietary pattern?
>> Probably. I guess I have to be honest
that's what I pretty much eat myself. So
that I guess if you look at sort of
voting with mouth that would be true.
But what we've realized is that now that
we've looked at a lot of other diets
around the world that many traditional
diets come pretty close to the
Mediterranean diet in terms of being
more plantforward, healthier fats and
can be similarly healthy but with
different foods and different flavors.
And so some times I enjoy meals from
Asia, Latin America, Africa, but really
fitting with the principles of the
Mediterranean diet. Also, you're
probably aware that the US News and
World Report does an annual survey among
nutritionists. I think they survey about
30 people and in the last eight years,
the Mediterranean diet has come out
number one as the top choice of
nutritionists in general. But again,
there's been a convergence. These
different dietary patterns like DASH,
which is also usually rated pretty high,
is pretty similar. Are there any myths
or misconceptions with regards to what
the Mediterranean diet actually looks
like, what it is in the science and when
it's being studied versus maybe maybe
public perception of what that diet
looks like? Well, yes, good point that I
usually try to insert the word
traditional in front of Mediterranean
diet because if you look at what it is
today, it's not what people were eating
in the 1960s when the initial surveys
that showed that in fact the
cardiovascular rates in the
Mediterranean, especially a cit
onetenth of the rates in Northern
Europe. At that time it was definitely
more plant-based than it is now. We red
meat consumption's gone way up. Dairy
consumption has gone up. And then we
have all Coca-Cola, McDonald's, very
widespread in the Mediterranean area. I
just got back from last several weeks in
Greece and you can still have really
good healthy food there, but that is not
what people are most people are eating
every day.
>> When you started out as a a university
student and you just said then that
you're you kind of vote with your mouth
and you eat a a sort of Mediterranean
style diet. Do you remember what your
diet looked like when your when you
began your career as a scientist and and
has it changed much over that time as
you've learned new things? It
>> certainly has changed. I grew up in
Michigan and Wisconsin, sort of the
heart of the dairy land. And so I grew
up uh recommendations were four glasses
of milk a day and I think I met that.
But it was uh also every day sometimes
several times a day red meat and mashed
potatoes were primary source of
carbohydrate in our diet. And
interestingly one of the reasons why if
you look at the number of calories per
American has increased so much. A lot of
this is what's available calories and
the drippings from the red meat that we
never threw those away. There were a lot
of calories there and we had them as
gravy on the and the mashed potatoes. So
there was uh butter but margarine too
some of the time that was that was
actually cheaper back then and very high
in trans fat. So yeah that was uh in
retrospect not a good diet. I guess I I
survived and [laughter]
[gasps] I just rode uh
80 miles in two days at my 80th birthday
80th birthday. So
>> happy birthday and congrats. [laughter]
>> Yeah. When my grandfather died at 68,
you know, farmer, really active. We
thought that was old. And when when I
started medical school, even people were
dropping dead, especially men at age 50,
55 from sudden cardiac death. And we we
called that just the big one. It was
almost normal that people would have
that. And we hardly ever see that
happening anymore. Also very
impressively, there have been uh several
studies done on soldiers killed in
action. And so they were usually late
teens, early 20s and looking at their
arteries back in the Korean War, they
were just loaded with aththerosclerosis
at that time. Even though they still
hadn't blocked their arteries, there was
some blood flow and they weren't having
heart attacks. They have to wait another
decade or two before the heart attacks
actually show up. And then there have
been several repeats of that done over
time and the more recent autopsy reports
show much less atherosclerosis plaque
buildup in arteries. Even at this young
age there's been a huge improvement.
>> You must have collaborated with you know
a number of nutrition scientists all
across the globe in your time over your
career. Would you say that most of them
who are interested in reducing their
risk of chronic disease are eating
similarly to you? I think that's pretty
true. Yes. Yeah, that's quite true. Even
some who raise questions, but when you
actually I think look at their plate, it
would be not very different. Some of my
colleagues are more enthusiastic or
quite enthusiastic about low
carbohydrate diets, but still even then
I think you can have a healthy low
carbohydrate diet if you're emphasizing
plant sources of protein primarily, not
necessarily strictly, but and also plant
sources of fats in the diet. So there's
some varying degrees about that shift to
overall macronutrients. But uh the good
thing is there's a lot of flexibility
that way and we can have a healthy diet
with quite a bit of variation in total
carbohydrate or total fat.
>> I'm interested to get to some of your
recent studies you've been involved in
that have looked at protein source and
protein amount and also some of the the
topics you've already brought up around
fat and omega sixes. Before we get
there, can you remind people about the
type of nutrition research that you've
been most involved in throughout your
career and what that's looked like?
>> When we started off back in the late
1970s, realizing we needed some data was
clear that was missing. We did have
studies, short-term studies of a few
dozen people, you feed them different
amounts of fats and you look at the
blood cholesterol levels, but clearly
diets more complicated than just the
type of fat and a diet. So if we were
going to make recommendations, it seemed
like we really needed evidence on what
people were eating and what happened to
them in over the following decades. And
so that what we call a prospective
epidemiologic study, we collected the
data while people were healthy and then
we track them over time. And because
people change their diets out of choice
and sometimes necessity and also the
food industry is changing what they
produce, we have updated the data every
four years as we've gone along. And
that's really been critically important
because we do see that people over time
do shift their diets. So we're able to
keep track of that and then along the
way keep track of their smoking habits,
physical activity, medication use, and
more recently genetics and family
history. So, we're keeping track of all
of that. Again, this would be called a
prospective epidemiologic study, but
importantly with repeated measurements.
>> And so, when you think about your
recommendations or even how you make
your own personal food choices, it's
based off of these long-term studies,
but also clinical trials. You're looking
at all of these different types of
evidence.
>> Absolutely. And that's really important
that when you really make decisions
about personal diets or policy and
recommendations, if you want the best
evidence, since we can't do the
theoretically perfect study of
randomizing people at birth to different
diets, that's not going to happen. The
best evidence will come from multiple
types of evidence and with repeated or
replicated measurements, replicated
findings. So for example for intake of
red meat we have the shortterm studies
again a few dozen people a few weeks
looking at meat intake and critically
important with a specified control
group. So it's always a nutrition it's
compared to what and the those kinds of
randomized studies do show that there
will be lower LDL bad cholesterol with
plant sources of protein such as nuts,
legumes, soy products compared to red
meat in the diet. And then we have our
long-term studies which show lower rates
of heart disease with among people who
have diets more emphasizing plant
protein sources compared to high amounts
of red meat. And so we put that kind of
evidence together and that can form
quite strong basis for guiding
individual diets or policy. And what
would you say to someone who is
generally skeptical about epidemiology?
And perhaps they're convinced that these
studies are not very helpful because in
the study where the person that's eating
more legumes or eating more soy foods,
eating less red meat, that particular
person is also likely the person who
exercises more, who drinks less alcohol,
who smokes less. So the fact that you
observe them over 20 or 30 years and
they have a lower risk of having a heart
attack or they have lower risk of
developing breast cancer that is
conffounded that may be just because
they are a person that makes healthy
choices overall and may not be explained
by the legumes or the food choices and
that's uh what we deal with in our very
first classes of introduction
epidemiology is confounding. So we pay a
lot of attention to that and we use
several different strategies to deal
with confounding. One is we call
restriction that although there's this
sort of popular idea we should include a
wide variety of people from all
different kinds of soio economic
backgrounds in our studies you really
open up the door to more confounding
when you do that. And so our studies
have mostly been in health professionals
and that decreases the range of
variation in education occupation major
major determinants or indicators of
socioeconomic status. you're already
dealing with more homogeneous population
which from an epidemiologic standpoint
is good unless you make it homogeneous
in the dietary factor that you want to
study then there's nothing to study if
everybody's eating the same but it turns
out if you look at registered nurses
there's a wide variation in what people
are eating still even though the
education and occupation is is similar
and then we gather detailed information
about smoking and physical activity and
everything we could think of that we
might want to measure that might
potentially be a confounding factor and
so we can adjust for that in our
statistical analyses. Then more recently
we've also been able to geocode where
people live as the average income things
like air pollution distances from fast
food places. We can adjust for all of
those factors. In fact, after we've done
everything else, those don't make much
difference. We've already control for
things. spouse's occupation can be
important to not just one's own income
and education that we can control for
that as well. So when we put these
layers of control and adjustment it does
pretty well. So actually what we what we
see is there's this idea that soio
economic status may be a confounder but
what's more factor what we find is that
instead of being a confounder diet is
actually explaining a lot of the
differences in socioeconomic status for
disease risk for example the restriction
strategy also allows us when we have a
big enough study a real concern about
smoking that's a strong risk factor for
so many diseases we can actually look at
people only at restrict an analysis to
people who never smoked any time in
their life. And when we see something
there, then essentially removes the
confounding by smoking.
>> And I think it's worth just emphasizing
to the listener that this is really no
different to looking at exercise and the
recommendations for how many minutes of
exercise someone does per week. There's
sure there's short-term exercise
clinical trials where you do an
intervention and look at how that
affects a short-term marker like
someone's V2 max, but then there is
longterm, you know, decades, 20, 30
years follow-up study that looks at a
kind of a cardiorespiratory fitness or
the number of minutes of exercise
someone's doing per week of moderate
intensity aerobic exercise for example.
And these researchers are faced with the
same thing. The person who is exercising
more may have other healthy behaviors.
And so a statistical adjustment is
required to try and best identify what
is the effect of that type of exercise
on whatever health outcome they're
looking at like cardiovascular disease.
>> Yeah, absolutely right. Whether it's
diet, physical activity, or other
behaviors, this kind of confounding will
always be an issue. when the best
information will come when you have
different kinds of studies short-term
long-term look at the whole weight of
evidence there.
>> I point that out just because I I feel
like it's seems to be more of an
objection against nutritional
epidemiology. I don't really hear people
saying the same thing about exercise
epidemiology and it it it might be just
because food is a little bit more
personal and and I guess debated than
than exercise whereas exercise is just
generally accepted as good right is
interesting that way there are some
areas where we can't be confident that
we've totally controlled for confounding
and we always have to keep in our mind
that potential especially actually the
biggest confounding factors are like to
be other aspects of diet that where we
see a general benefit of say fruit and
